subject: Chemistry
title: Karen Wetterhahn: A Drop of Dimethylmercury
description: A quick, practical guide to Karen Wetterhahn: A Drop of Dimethylmercury.

hook.text: In 1996, a single drop of dimethylmercury fell on Karen Wetterhahn's glove. She died ten months later.

buildup.text: Wetterhahn was a chemistry professor at Dartmouth studying toxic metals. She wore standard latex gloves and a lab coat.

discovery.text: Dimethylmercury passes through latex gloves in seconds. It penetrates skin instantly and attacks the nervous system.

twist.text: Symptoms appeared five months later: slurred speech, loss of balance, blindness. By then, mercury had destroyed her brain.

climax.text: Her death changed lab safety worldwide. Thicker laminate gloves and fume hoods are now required for organomercury compounds.

punchline.text: One drop through a glove. The most toxic organic mercury compound killed an expert.

quiz.question: Why was dimethylmercury so dangerous despite Wetterhahn wearing gloves?
options: It passes through standard latex gloves in seconds and absorbs through skin instantly
The glove had an invisible tear that she didn't notice
She accidentally inhaled vapors when she removed the glove
